target.title: fix(agents): ensure inline image attachments use ASCII base64 (Fixes #86984)
target.body:
## Summary

Inline and replayed-history image attachments handed in by channel plugins can carry a raw latin1/binary byte string in their `data` field. This value was forwarded untouched into the provider `source.base64`. The Anthropic Messages API rejects any request whose `image.source.base64` contains non-ASCII characters:
messages.337.content.1.image.source.base64: string argument should contain only ASCII characters

Because history images are re-hydrated on every turn, a single poisoned history image block silently breaks every subsequent turn in a long-lived session, and the agent goes silent with isError=true. The same raw passthrough also reaches the OpenAI/Responses image mappers.

## Root cause

`resolveAgentTurnAttachments` (current-turn path) already encodes file buffers via `buffer.toString("base64")`. But `resolveInlineAgentImageAttachments` forwarded `data` untouched:

```ts
.map((image) => ({ mediaType: image.mimeType, data: image.data }))
```

When `data` holds unencoded latin1 bytes, the downstream provider mapper builds the image source with non-ASCII content and the API rejects the whole turn.

## Fix

`resolveInlineAgentImageAttachments` now routes `data` through an idempotent `ensureAsciiBase64` guard built on the existing `canonicalizeBase64` helper (the same one used by `tool-images.ts`):

- Valid base64 is always ASCII, so `canonicalizeBase64` returns it unchanged and correct payloads pass through untouched.
- Only non-base64 payloads are re-encoded from their latin1 bytes via `Buffer.from(data, "latin1").toString("base64")`.

The guard is purely defensive and idempotent, so normal images are unaffected.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Inline/replayed-history image attachments with raw latin1 data produced a non-ASCII provider source.base64, which the Anthropic API rejected, breaking every subsequent turn (issue 86984).

Real environment tested: Local Ubuntu 24.04 (WSL2), Node v24.14.0, exercising the actual resolveInlineAgentImageAttachments entry point on disk.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the command shown in the evidence below against the real source file.

Evidence after fix: I ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-turn-attachments.test.ts` directly on this machine. The captured terminal output below is from that real `node` invocation. To confirm the regression is genuinely detected, the same `node` command was run with the one-line change temporarily reverted to the original passthrough (BEFORE), then restored (AFTER):
-- BEFORE (data: image.data passthrough), node run on this host
auto-reply  agent-turn-attachments.test.ts (4 tests | 1 failed)
x   re-encodes raw latin1/binary data into ASCII base64
AssertionError: expected false to be true
at expect(ASCII_ONLY.test(attachment.data)).toBe(true)
Test Files  1 failed (1)
Tests       1 failed | 3 passed (4)
-- AFTER (data: ensureAsciiBase64(image.data)), node run on this host
auto-reply  agent-turn-attachments.test.ts (4 tests)
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       4 passed (4)
Duration    315ms

Observed result after fix: the `node` run reports all 4 cases green. The latin1 payload is re-encoded to valid ASCII base64 and decodes back to the original image bytes; already-valid base64 is returned unchanged (idempotent); non-image and empty attachments are still filtered out.

What was not tested: the same raw-data passthrough also exists on the Anthropic and OpenAI/Responses provider mappers. This change fixes the single inline-attachment chokepoint that all inline/replayed images flow through; a redundant guard at the provider-mapper layer is left out to keep the change minimal and can be added separately if maintainers prefer defense in depth. Live end-to-end reproduction against a real provider API was not performed.

target.title: UI: fix half-block art rendering for UTF-8 QR codes in web chat
target.body:
## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: UTF-8 QR codes (using half-block characters ▀▄█) render broken in the web chat dashboard — default `
` line-height creates gaps between rows, background/foreground colors don't match the block-character encoding, and the characters don't tile into a scannable image.
- Why it matters: QR codes sent by tools (e.g. Algorand ARC-26 QR generator) are unreadable/unscannable in the web UI, forcing users to copy raw text and render elsewhere.
- What changed: Added `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` detection in `markdown.ts` that identifies code blocks dominated by half-block Unicode characters, wraps them with a `.half-block-art` CSS class, and applies tight line-height (0.6), black background, white foreground, and slightly larger font-size so the blocks tile seamlessly. CSS added for both `.chat-text` (chat messages) and `.sidebar-markdown` (tool output sidebar), with both light and dark theme variants.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to the markdown parsing pipeline, sanitization, or any other code block rendering. Only half-block-art detection and styling were added.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Code blocks containing UTF-8 half-block art (QR codes, box-drawing art) now render with black background, white text, and tight line-height so they display as intended scannable images.
- No change to normal code block rendering.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS, Ubuntu
- Runtime/container: TUI, Browser (web chat dashboard)
- Model/provider: Any (content is tool output)
- Integration/channel (if any): Web UI
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Open the web chat dashboard
2. Have a tool or assistant send a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. via Algorand `generate_algorand_qrcode` or any tool that outputs Unicode block-character QR codes)
3. Observe the code block rendering

### Expected

- QR code renders as a clean, scannable square image with no gaps between rows

### Actual

- Before: rows have visible gaps due to default line-height, background is gray instead of black, characters don't tile — QR is unreadable
- After: QR code renders correctly and is scannable

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: UTF-8 QR codes built from half-block characters (▀▄█) rendered broken in the web chat dashboard — the default `
` line-height left gaps between rows and the gray background/foreground did not match the block-character encoding, so the codes were unscannable. This patch detects half-block art, wraps it in a `.half-block-art` class, and applies tight line-height, black background, white foreground, and larger font-size so the blocks tile into a clean scannable image, across both the chat-text and sidebar surfaces in light and dark themes.

Real environment tested: OpenClaw web chat dashboard running locally on macOS and Ubuntu (Node 22), rendering live tool output that emits a UTF-8 QR code (e.g. the Algorand ARC-26 `generate_algorand_qrcode` tool).

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Opened the web chat dashboard, had a tool emit a UTF-8 half-block QR code, then viewed it in both the chat message view and the tool-output sidebar, in light and dark themes, and scanned the rendered code with a phone camera.

Evidence after fix:

**Before**



**After**





Observed result after fix: The QR code renders as a clean, gap-free square and scans correctly with a phone camera in both the chat-text and sidebar surfaces; normal code blocks remain unaffected.

What was not tested: Terminal-only (TUI) rendering paths and exhaustive QR color combinations; the detection heuristic may not cover every possible block-art edge case.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: QR code rendering in chat messages and tool output sidebar, both light and dark themes
- Edge cases checked: Normal code blocks unaffected, short block-character snippets (< 10 lines) do not trigger detection, non-QR code blocks render as before
- What you did **not** verify: All possible QRCode coloring; detection heuristic may not cover all edge cases of block art

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the `.half-block-art` CSS rules in `text.css` and `sidebar.css`, and remove the `looksLikeHalfBlockArt()` branch in `markdown.ts`
- Files/config to restore: `ui/src/styles/chat/text.css`, `ui/src/styles/chat/sidebar.css`, `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Normal code blocks accidentally styled as half-block art (false positive detection), or QR codes still rendering with gaps (false negative)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: False positive detection on code blocks that happen to contain many block characters but aren't QR/art
  - Mitigation: Detection requires 60%+ of lines to contain half-block characters AND at least 10 lines — unlikely for normal code. Worst case is cosmetic (black bg + tight line-height on a non-QR block).

target.title: feat(security/exec): add tools.exec.denyPathPatterns hard-deny gate (#74379)
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an opt-in `tools.exec.denyPathPatterns` — a config-level deny gate that blocks `exec` / `system.run` invocations whose path arguments match configured glob patterns, **before** allowlist/approval/safe-bin checks. A denied path (e.g. `~/.openclaw/secrets/**`, `**/.ssh/id_*`) cannot be reached via `security="full"`, allow-always, or safe-bins.

- New `src/infra/exec-deny-path.ts`: glob compilation, home-dir expansion, cwd-relative resolution, and best-effort POSIX shell `-c` payload tokenization.
- Wired into both the bash-tools exec path (`src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts`) and the node-host `system.run` path (`src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts`).
- Global + per-agent patterns are unioned (agents can extend but not relax the deny list), resolved in `src/agents/agent-tools.ts`.

Fixes #74379.

This is a fresh rework of #81827 against current `main`: the deny-gate config wiring moved from the now-removed `pi-tools.ts` to `agent-tools.ts`, and the base config schema is no longer a committed generated file. No `CHANGELOG.md` entry (release-owned).

### Addressing the prior review (P1s)

- **Windows path separators preserved**: the shell-payload tokenizer no longer treats `\` as an escape on `win32`, so Windows-style path tokens survive matching.
- **Wrapper-hidden payloads on the node-host path**: a dedicated `extractShellWrappedPayloadFromArgv` recovers a `sh -c` payload from `argv` (main's `system.run` parse leaves it there), so paths hidden inside a shell wrapper are matched on that path too. (It deliberately mirrors `extractShellWrappedCommandPayload` in `bash-tools.exec.ts` rather than sharing it: the bash-tools helper is also used by the unrelated interpreter-preflight path and takes a pre-split `(executable, args)`, so unifying them would tangle two security paths for no net reduction.)
- **Honest v1 scope**: documented (help text + `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md`) that v1 is a hard-deny for literal path-like arguments after simple shell-quote stripping, and does **not** defeat heredocs, eval-style indirection, base64-decoded paths, command substitution, symlink redirection, or `cmd.exe`/PowerShell wrappers.

## Real behavior proof

ClawSweeper asked for proof from the **real configured exec gate run outside the Vitest/mock harness**. Below is exactly that: a throwaway `node --import tsx` harness (not committed) that imports the *production* deny-gate functions and composes them **identically to the node-host `system.run` gate** (`src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts:516-537`) — `resolveExecDenyPathPatterns` → `extractShellWrappedPayloadFromArgv` → `evaluateExecDenyPathMatch` — with zero mocking, against a realistic operator config. The full end-to-end "host executor is never called" property is additionally covered by the Vitest path suite (which mocks only the downstream host executor, legitimately).

Behavior addressed: a hard-deny path gate that fires before allowlist/approval/safe-bins, including for a documented `**/.env` example on the no-cwd node-host path, not bypassable via POSIX shell wrappers or Windows separators, and failing toward checking (never open) on malformed shell input.
Real environment tested: macOS (arm64), node 22, openclaw source at this branch HEAD (`b73275c1a`); deny-gate code run unmocked via `node --import tsx`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: composed the real node-host gate (`resolveExecDenyPathPatterns({global:["**/.env","**/.ssh/*"], agent:["~/.ssh/id_*"]})` → `evaluateExecDenyPathMatch` with `shellPayload: extractShellWrappedPayloadFromArgv(argv)`) in a throwaway `exec-deny-realproof.mts` (deleted, not committed), run with `node --import tsx exec-deny-realproof.mts`; plus the Vitest path/system.run suites for the host-never-called assertion.
Evidence after fix: unmocked production deny-gate output (paths/keys are synthetic placeholders, no real secrets):

```
$ node --import tsx exec-deny-realproof.mts
configured denyPathPatterns: ["**/.env","**/.ssh/*","~/.ssh/id_*"]

=== real node-host deny-gate composition (unmocked production functions) ===
DENIED  | cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa  (no cwd)
        | SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: ... (pattern="**/.ssh/*", arg="~/.ssh/id_rsa")
DENIED  | cat .env  (no cwd)                         <- prior review gap, now fixed
        | SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: ... (pattern="**/.env", arg=".env")
DENIED  | sh -lc 'cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519'  (argv-recovered wrapper payload)
        | SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: ... (pattern="**/.ssh/*", arg="cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519")
DENIED  | type C:\Users\<redacted>\.ssh\id_rsa  (Windows-style argv token)
        | SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: ... (pattern="**/.ssh/*", arg="C:\\Users\\<redacted>\\.ssh\\id_rsa")
DENIED  | cat config/.env  (cwd=/srv/app)
        | SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: ... (pattern="**/.env", arg="config/.env")
ALLOWED | cat README.md  (control)
DENIED  | malformed-quote raw payload fallback (unbalanced quote -> splitShellArgs null)
        | SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: ... (pattern="**/.ssh/*", arg="~/.ssh/id_rsa")
```

Plus, the end-to-end exec tool denies and never reaches the host executor:
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.test.ts
 ✓ hard-denies a bare relative deny target on the node host even when workdir is omitted
     (nodeHostMocks.executeNodeHostCommand NOT called)
 ✓ still denies a deny target when shell tokenization fails (unbalanced quote)   <- new fail-closed regression
 ✓ denies configured path patterns before allowlist or execution  (reason: "deny-path-pattern")
```

Observed result after fix: the unmocked production deny-gate (same composition as the node-host `system.run` path) hard-denies every targeted command and allows the control. The previously-flagged gap — `**/.env` not blocking `cat .env` without a cwd — is fixed: `**/` is a globstar matching zero leading segments, so bare relative candidates match. A Windows-style argv token (`C:\...\.ssh\id_rsa`) matches the POSIX `**/.ssh/*` pattern because `normalizeMatchTarget` rewrites `\`→`/` on every host. Malformed shell input (unbalanced quote) where `splitShellArgs` returns null still denies via the raw-payload fallback — the gate fails toward checking, never open. The Vitest path/system.run suites confirm the downstream host executor is never invoked and the deny reason is emitted before the allowlist.
What was not tested: live `cmd.exe`/PowerShell wrapper parsing (explicitly out of v1 scope and documented), and non-path indirection (heredocs, base64, command substitution) which v1 does not claim to defeat.

## Automated tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-deny-path.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools-agent-config.exec.test.ts
 Test Files  4 passed (4)
      Tests  113 passed (113)
```

(Count reflects the post-review tests: new Windows-argv-token, cross-platform fake-home, and fail-closed malformed-quote regressions added; some prior over-counted shards consolidated.) Lint: `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the changed files → clean.

## Risks

Medium (security boundary). The gate is opt-in (no behavior change unless `denyPathPatterns` is configured) and fails closed. v1 limitations are documented so operators do not over-trust it as a complete sandbox. Maintainer sign-off on the v1 semantics is welcome.

target.title: ACP: add gateway-owned node-backed runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker.
- Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove.
- What this PR adds:
  - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints
  - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes
  - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing
  - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator
  - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs
  - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage
  - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend
- Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes.

## What changed by area

### 1) Durable ACP gateway state

New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`:

- persists ACP sessions and runs
- persists accepted worker events
- tracks leases / lease epochs
- resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway
- stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor

This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node.

### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/**`
- `src/infra/node-commands.ts`
- `src/node-host/**`

This adds the node-backed ACP execution path:

- gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node
- node-host can run the ACP worker role
- worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport
- gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution

### 3) Durable reply projection and replay

Main implementation areas:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`

This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path:

- reply targets are persisted per run
- projector checkpoints are advanced only after successful delivery
- restart recovery can replay the unfinished suffix from durable state
- reconnect and duplicate accepted events do not re-deliver already completed outward effects

### 4) Startup recovery / reconnect handling

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
- `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`

This adds the gateway-side recovery flow needed to:

- resume incomplete projection after restart
- keep reconnect handling deterministic
- prevent stale-worker wins
- preserve canonical terminal behavior across restart/replay windows

### 5) Test harnesses and regression coverage

Main test additions/expansions:

- `src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts`
- `src/acp/test-harness/restart-harness.ts`

These cover the core failure windows this backend needs to survive:

- restart after accepted events but before replay checkpoint advancement
- restart after terminal persistence but before final outward convergence
- reconnect replay of already accepted events
- cancel / complete / close race handling
- restart recovery with a real memory cut instead of reusing in-process objects

### 6) Docs included in this PR

Main docs:

- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-verification.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-runtime-program.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-summary.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-final-judge.md`

These are included because this was developed as a staged architecture / verification effort and the final accepted behavior is documented there.

## Explicit scope boundaries / v1 limitations

This PR does **not**:

- turn nodes into mini-gateways
- add automatic cross-node continuation for an in-flight run
- claim durable resumed tool-message edit support after restart

Current conservative post-restart tool-update behavior remains:

- `append_only_after_restart`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: ACP node runtime architecture / protocol / verification docs under `docs/experiments/plans/`
- Related: landing-only extension test isolation fixes after rebase onto current `main`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenClaw now has a first-class ACP node-backed runtime path where the gateway owns durable ACP truth and paired nodes execute the worker role.
- Gateway restart/recovery can resume incomplete ACP outward projection from durable state.
- Duplicate accepted worker-event replay on reconnect is hardened so it does not duplicate outward delivery.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally follow the documented conservative v1 rule: `append_only_after_restart`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? Yes

### Security / trust-boundary notes

- Paired nodes can now advertise ACP worker capability and receive ACP runtime commands over the existing gateway↔node trust boundary.
- Gateway↔node websocket traffic now carries ACP worker control / event flows in addition to existing node commands.
- Gateway now persists ACP runtime state needed for durable replay/recovery.
- The design mitigates stale-worker / duplicate-delivery risks via lease epochs, canonical terminal resolution, durable replay checkpoints, and reconnect/replay handling on the gateway.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (landing worktree on vm1) + GitHub Actions CI
- Runtime/container: Node / pnpm; Bun build path also checked
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP gateway↔node runtime path
- Relevant config (redacted): ACP runtime restart/replay harnesses and paired-node fixtures

### Verified locally

- ACP targeted verification gate:
  - `pnpm test -- src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts src/acp/store/store.test.ts src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts src/acp/store/gateway-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.acp.test.ts`
- TypeScript guardrail:
  - `pnpm tsgo`
- Landing-only extension test fixes:
  - `pnpm test:extensions`

### CI / build notes

- `pnpm build` still fails only with the pre-existing Bun wrapper issue:
  - `Cannot find module './cjs/index.cjs' from ''`
- That build failure was already present and is not introduced by the ACP runtime changes in this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the ACP-focused local gate listed above.
- Verified `pnpm tsgo` passes.
- Verified the rebased landing branch’s extension test-isolation fixes with local `pnpm test:extensions`.
- Verified the PR now includes the architecture / protocol / verification / final-judge docs for this backend.

What I did **not** verify:

- I did **not** get a clean `pnpm build` because of the pre-existing Bun wrapper failure noted above.
- I did **not** run a fresh manual end-to-end Discord/real-node interactive repro outside the checked-in restart/replay and node-host harnesses.

## Review Guide

If you want the shortest path through this PR, review in this order:

1. **Core architecture / intent**
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
2. **Gateway-owned durable state**
   - `src/acp/store/**`
3. **Node execution bridge**
   - `src/node-host/**`
   - `src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
4. **Durable outward replay path**
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`
5. **Recovery and proofs**
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
6. **Ignore as landing-only test fixes unless you are checking CI fallout**
   - `extensions/feishu/**`
   - `extensions/google/**`
   - `extensions/googlechat/**`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No explicit migration required
- Migration needed? No

Notes:

- This adds a new ACP runtime/backend path; it does not force existing deployments to switch to it immediately.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally remain append-only in v1.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR or avoid selecting/using the `acp-node` runtime path until fixed.
- Files/config to restore: ACP runtime/store/gateway/node-host changes under `src/acp/**`, `src/node-host/**`, `src/gateway/**`, plus the accompanying docs if reverting wholesale.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate final delivery after restart, stale worker winning after reconnect, projector checkpoint drift, or replay targeting the wrong route.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: restart/replay correctness is subtle in any durable projection system.
  - Mitigation: this PR includes restart harnesses, startup recovery tests, reconnect/replay ordering tests, and explicit protocol/verification docs.
- Risk: the PR is large and spans multiple ACP runtime layers.
  - Mitigation: the review guide above splits it into durable store, node bridge, replay path, recovery path, and landing-only CI fixes.
- Risk: paired-node ACP execution expands the gateway↔node command surface.
  - Mitigation: the gateway remains authoritative for leases, terminal resolution, checkpoints, and replay instead of trusting node-local completion.

---

## AI Checklist

- [x] **AI-assisted** — agent-harness / Codex driven implementation, review, and landing follow-ups
- [x] **Testing degree** — focused local verification plus CI
- [x] **Understanding confirmed** — this PR adds the gateway-owned ACP node-backed runtime and includes a few landing-only extension test fixes after rebase
- [ ] Prompts/session logs — available on request

target.title: feat(discord): show subagent progress
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Discord users currently have little visible feedback when an assistant turn delegates work to tracked sub-agents. Long-running turns can look idle after the initial message even though background sub-agents are still running, and a sub-agent failure is not visible on the original Discord message.

Discord also cannot represent concurrent work by adding the same emoji reaction multiple times from the same bot/user, so progress needs a count-based reaction model instead of repeated identical reactions.

## What This Adds

This PR adds Discord-scoped, best-effort sub-agent progress feedback on the original inbound Discord message. The feature is opt-in with `channels.discord.subagentProgress.enabled=true`.

When enabled:

- one configurable count reaction shows the number of currently active tracked sub-agents (`1️⃣` … `🔟` by default)
- count changes replace the previous count reaction instead of accumulating multiple ordinal reactions
- zero active tracked sub-agents removes the count reaction
- any non-OK completion leaves a durable configurable failure marker (`🔴` by default)
- Discord typing is kept alive independently while tracked sub-agents remain active, then stops when all tracked work finishes or the safety TTL expires

The implementation threads the source Discord message id through native `sessions_spawn`, tracked ACP spawn, requester origin metadata, and internal Discord progress hook handling so the Discord extension can target the correct parent message. Non-Discord callers and callers without a source message id keep existing behavior.

## User Impact

Discord users get immediate, lightweight progress feedback during delegated work without waiting for the final assistant reply:

- `1️⃣`, `2️⃣`, etc. show the current active tracked sub-agent count for the turn
- the count reaction is replaced as the active count changes
- the count reaction disappears when no tracked sub-agents remain active
- `🔴` remains if any tracked sub-agent ends with a non-OK outcome
- typing stays active while tracked sub-agents are still running

Operators can configure the behavior with `channels.discord.subagentProgress`, including reaction progress, count emoji, failure emoji, typing keepalive, interval, and max-duration TTL.

The feature is best-effort and Discord-only. Reaction or typing API failures are debug-logged and do not block spawn/completion flow.

## Implementation Notes

- Adds source/current message id propagation through delivery context and `sessions_spawn` tool context.
- Carries that id into native sub-agent spawn and tracked ACP registration.
- Keeps public plugin hook/requester metadata narrow while the Discord extension uses an internal Discord-only event shape for source-message progress targeting.
- Emits progress cleanup at run completion even when ordinary `subagent_ended` delivery is deferred.
- Adds a Discord progress tracker keyed by account id, parent channel id, typing/thread target id, and source message id to avoid thread collisions.
- Tracks active run ids and derives the displayed count from current active set size.
- Replaces the displayed count reaction when the active count changes, removes it at zero, and caps counts above the configured emoji list to the final configured emoji.
- Uses `unref` on typing timers where supported so keepalive intervals do not keep Node alive.
- Updates Discord config schema, exported config types, UI hints, generated channel config metadata, and docs.

## Evidence

### Rebase/squash proof — 2026-06-23 UTC

Latest pushed commit after same-base rebase and squash: `e8c72c3ec83ecd1c68702bf48e5aa44be5887f70`.

All six coordinated PR branches in this batch were rebased onto the same pinned upstream base commit:

```text
dc9c11be917ebdc711b956250aa80a8e5b47bea6
```

Post-rebase local verification included conflict-marker/diff hygiene plus focused branch-specific gates. Final proof marker for this PR:

```text
POST_REBASE_20260623_DISCORD_SUBAGENT_PROGRESS_GATES_OK
```

Focused validation passed locally on branch `feature/discord-subagent-progress`:

```bash
pnpm test extensions/discord/src/subagent-hooks.test.ts \
  extensions/discord/src/config-schema.test.ts \
  src/plugins/wired-hooks-subagent.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts \
  src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts
```

This verifies:

- source/current message id propagation through delivery context and spawn paths
- tracked ACP/native sub-agent progress hook behavior
- default-off behavior when `subagentProgress.enabled` is missing or false
- enabled Discord count reaction add/replace/remove behavior
- failure marker behavior for non-OK completion
- per-thread tracker separation and typing target behavior
- count capping above the configured ordinal list
- progress cleanup for deferred completion paths
- narrow public hook metadata behavior
- Discord config schema validation

Static/config/docs gates passed locally:

```bash
pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false
pnpm config:channels:check
pnpm docs:list
git diff --check
```

## Manual Live Gate

Live Discord API proof was run from the installed PR stack on Dev-01 using `OpenClaw 2026.6.10-beta.1` at local HEAD `f15c88bd6f9 stack: combine beta memory slots and Discord progress`, with:

- `channels.discord.subagentProgress.enabled=true`
- `channels.discord.subagentProgress.reactions.enabled=true`
- `channels.discord.subagentProgress.typing.enabled=true`

Observed live behavior in Discord:

- **Normal channel success:** a Discord-originated parent turn spawned a tracked sub-agent run (`8c6dbfa0-e021-4c3a-9992-328de22caa13`). The source Discord message showed the active count reaction while the child was running, typing stayed active during child work, and the count reaction was removed after the child completed. Child proof line: `NORMAL_SUCCESS_1_DONE 2026-06-22T04:48:33Z`.
- **Thread-bound success:** a thread-bound Discord sub-agent run (`01fa6443-4fc1-409d-9cf3-969ce433edbd`) completed successfully while progress was shown from the Discord parent context. Child proof line: `THREAD_SUCCESS_1_DONE 2026-06-22T04:49:50Z`.
- **Concurrent count replacement:** two overlapping tracked sub-agent runs (`4b2701ef-85b0-41d5-a87f-f72924e21869` and `9a5b1e70-5b3b-4820-bb5f-cb6cb7b2a926`) exercised count add/replace/remove behavior. The Discord source message count reaction incremented while both children were active, decremented as the first child completed, and cleared after the second child completed. Child proof lines: `CONCURRENT_SUCCESS_2_DONE 2026-06-22T05:06:23Z` and `CONCURRENT_SUCCESS_3_DONE 2026-06-22T05:06:36Z`.
- **Typing keepalive/stop:** Discord typing was visible while tracked sub-agents were active and stopped after tracked work drained. Earlier long-running proof passes also hit the configured typing safety TTL, with gateway logs showing `[typing] TTL exceeded (60000ms), auto-stopping typing indicator`, confirming the timeout guard executes.

Additional observation: after the final child drains, there can be a brief visual gap before the parent posts its final reconciliation response. That appears to be parent-yield lifecycle behavior rather than this PR's sub-agent progress tracker; it is being tracked separately as a follow-up investigation and is not treated as a blocker for this PR.

Additional durable failure-marker proof was captured from a real Discord source message after a true non-OK sub-agent lifecycle:

- **Failure marker:** source message `1518528276031602788` in Discord channel `1507934992699756645` retained the configured durable failure reaction `🔴` with count `1` and `me: true` after the non-OK sub-agent lifecycle. The source message timestamp was `2026-06-22T08:09:13.897Z`. A later follow-up heartbeat at `2026-06-22T08:29:48.355Z` reported a separate verification pass, confirming the red marker was left on the original source message rather than being ordinary final text delivery.

Redacted observation:

```json
{
  "channel_id": "1507934992699756645",
  "message_id": "1518528276031602788",
  "timestampUtc": "2026-06-22T08:09:13.897Z",
  "content": "Extensively check over the branch end-to-end against the recommended fix and prior investigation/research to ensure everything is clean, finalized, and polished.",
  "reactions": [
    {
      "emoji": { "name": "🔴" },
      "count": 1,
      "me": true
    }
  ]
}
```

## PR/Commit Surface Breakdown

Source: 29 files, +846 / -81
Tests: 11 files, +1232 / -20
Total: 40 files, +2078 / -101

## Rebase / Proof Refresh (2026-06-23)

- Base: `654544b6b7c49d78afbacacd4861bcc830172780`
- Head after refresh: `2be5cc3716a5515ac432c763eff1db472d7e32c9`
- Conflict resolution: Resolved `scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs` budget conflict by keeping the PR-added SDK surface counts on top of current `main`.
- Validation: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-vitest-cache-pr95604 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/discord/src/subagent-hooks.test.ts extensions/discord/src/config-schema.test.ts src/utils/delivery-context.test.ts` -> passed, 3 files / 77 tests across 2 shards.
- Push: force-with-lease to `kklouzal/openclaw:feature/discord-subagent-progress` completed.

commands.author: jason-allen-oneal

target.title: fix(session-write-lock): enable reclaim of locks held past holder's own maxHoldMs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The session write lock's reclaim mechanism treated `"hold-exceeded"` (past the holder's own `maxHoldMs`) as report-only, meaning a contending process could never remove a lock that was overdue by the holder's own declared contract. Combined with the fact that the in-process watchdog (`setInterval`) cannot fire when the holder's event loop is wedged, this caused session locks to pin indefinitely — producing `session file locked (timeout 60000ms)` for every concurrent writer until the gateway was restarted.

The fix removes `"hold-exceeded"` from `REPORT_ONLY_STALE_LOCK_REASONS`. A holder past its **own recorded `maxHoldMs`** is overdue by its own contract and is now reclaimable by a contender. The conservative `"too-old"` (global `staleMs`) stays report-only — a live holder merely past the global heuristic but within its own `maxHoldMs` is still respected.

A secondary guard fix: `shouldRetryStaleAcquireFailure` now checks `sessionLockHeldByThisProcess` when the lock file is missing, preventing an infinite retry loop when the lock is actually held by this process.

### Compatibility

- Operators who configured `session.writeLock.maxHoldMs` or `OPENCLAW_SESSION_WRITE_LOCK_MAX_HOLD_MS` will see the value preserved in the lock file. The change only affects what happens when a lock exceeds that value: contenders can now reclaim it.
- The `remove-if-unchanged` recovery still skips a lock whose file changed (e.g. a concurrent release), so only a genuinely stalled holder loses the lock.
- The `cleanStaleLockFiles` doctor sweep does not pass `respectMaxHold` and is unchanged.

## Evidence

Deterministic regression tests in `src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts`:

```text
reclaims a live OpenClaw-owned lock that exceeded its own maxHoldMs
does NOT reclaim a live OpenClaw-owned lock merely past global staleMs but within its own maxHoldMs
```

Run after the patch:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts
  ✓  agents  src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts (55 tests) 386ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests  55 passed (55)
```

The new reclaim case fails on current `main` exactly as the issue describes — `SessionWriteLockStaleError: session file lock stale (hold-exceeded): … alive=true` — and passes after dropping `"hold-exceeded"` from the report-only set, while all existing cases (including the conservative "report live too-old locks without removing them") stay green.

### Observed result (overdue live holder reclaimed; conservative live holder respected)

```text
=== Test 1: Reclaim a live OpenClaw-owned lock past its own maxHoldMs ===
  Lock reclaimed: createdAt is 0ms old (< 5000ms)
  PASS

=== Test 2: Do NOT reclaim a live lock within its own maxHoldMs ===
  Correctly rejected: session file lock stale (too-old): pid=... alive=true ageMs=30001
  PASS
```

### Lock payload before/after

Before:
```json
{"pid": 1302, "createdAt": "…", "maxHoldMs": 300000}
```

After:
```json
{"pid": 1302, "createdAt": "…", "maxHoldMs": 300000}
```

The `maxHoldMs` value itself is unchanged — only the reclaim behavior when that deadline is exceeded changes. A contender that previously saw an unrecoverable stale lock now successfully reclaims and re-acquires.

## Changes

- `src/agents/session-write-lock.ts`:
  - drop `"hold-exceeded"` from `REPORT_ONLY_STALE_LOCK_REASONS` (one element + comment).
  - guard the missing-pid retry with `!sessionLockHeldByThisProcess` to avoid spinning on our own lock.
- `src/agents/session-write-lock.test.ts`:
  - regression test: a live OpenClaw-owned lock past its own `maxHoldMs` (but within `staleMs`) is reclaimed.
  - regression test: a live OpenClaw-owned lock merely past global `staleMs` (within its `maxHoldMs`) is NOT reclaimed.

## Reproduction

Issue #87483: a session write-lock held by a **live** gateway process persisted for 8+ hours past `maxHoldMs`/`staleMs`; the watchdog never reclaimed it, so later requests failed with `session file locked (timeout 60000ms): pid=…`. The reporter's holder process was alive and busy (83% CPU) while holding the lock despite the lock recording `maxHoldMs: 1020000` (17 min).

### Before fix

1. A long-running gateway holds a session write-lock and gets stuck in a synchronous CPU loop (event loop blocked).
2. The holder's in-process watchdog can't fire, so the lock is never self-released past `maxHoldMs`.
3. Another request tries to write the same session; it sees the lock past the holder's `maxHoldMs` but cannot reclaim it because `shouldRemoveContendedLockFile` treats `"hold-exceeded"` as report-only.
4. The contender fails with a stale/`session file locked` error; the lock persists until the gateway restarts or the file is deleted manually.

### After fix

The contender reclaims the lock (the stalled holder's overdue lock is removed-if-unchanged) and proceeds.

## Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: reclaiming a live holder's lock could race with a holder still writing the transcript.
  **Mitigation**: no new race is introduced. The target case is a *wedged* holder whose event loop is blocked — it cannot be writing the transcript (transcript writes are async and need the event loop). A holder that is still making progress already releases at `maxHoldMs` by its own in-process watchdog, so `maxHoldMs` was already the hard hold limit before this change; the cross-process reclaim only restores that limit for the wedged case where the watchdog cannot fire. `remove-if-unchanged` additionally skips a lock whose file changed.
- **Risk**: behavior change in session-state concurrency.
  **Mitigation**: the change narrows report-only to the global `staleMs` heuristic; it does not touch thresholds, the watchdog, or the doctor sweep, and the conservative live-`too-old` behavior is preserved (covered by an existing test).

target.title: [Bug]: google-vertex in openclaw doesn't recognize the latest models
target.body:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

After upgrading from 2026.5.20 to 2026.6.10 google-vertex does not recognize Gemini 3.5 flash mode. When trying to use it, it throws an error.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Setup google enterprise (vertex) ADC
2. Configure the model `google-vertex/gemini-3.5-flash`
3. Try to use it and it will throw an error


### Expected behavior

It should work

### Actual behavior

⚠️ Agent failed before reply: Unknown model: google-vertex/gemini-3.5-flash. Found agents.defaults.models["google-vertex/gemini-3.5-flash"], but no matching models.providers["google-vertex"].models[] entry. Add { "id": "gemini-3.5-flash", "name": "gemini-3.5-flash" } to models.providers["google-vertex"].models[] to register this provider model. For custom or proxy providers, also set api and baseUrl so requests route to the intended endpoint. See https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/model-providers.
Logs: openclaw logs --follow

### OpenClaw version

2026.6.10

### Operating system

Ubuntu 26.04

### Install method

npm global

### Model

gemini 3.5 flash

### Provider / routing chain

openclaw -> gemini adc

### Additional provider/model setup details

_No response_

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell

```

### Impact and severity

Affected: everyone using ADC
Severity: unusable
Frequency: always
Consequence: model is unusable

### Additional information
Gemini 2.5 flash works, it doesn't seem to recognize that gemini 3.5 flash is a valid model

_No response_

commands.author: ZengWen-DT

target.title: fix(memory): keep shared QMD manager alive on active-memory recall timeout
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96455. After an `active-memory` embedded recall run times out, chat-session `memory_search` returns `"memory search manager is closed"` even though `openclaw memory search --agent main` keeps working against the same QMD backend. The first closed-manager result can also trip the 60s `memory_search` cooldown, so recovery looks sticky until the gateway restarts.

Root cause: active-memory's recall-timeout cleanup (`scheduleMemorySearchCleanupAfterTimeout`) called `closeActiveMemorySearchManager`, which fully retired the **agent-scoped shared QMD manager** that chat `memory_search` and the startup timers/watchers depend on. A request-scoped recall timeout was tearing down long-lived shared state.

## Why This Change Was Made

The cleanup's legitimate job (introduced in #84048) is to release the **disposable local builtin index/embedding providers** a hung recall can leave open — not to retire the shared manager. The fix keeps the shipped close contract intact and does not grow the SDK surface:

- `closeActiveMemorySearchManager` gains a backward-compatible optional `scope?: "manager" | "index-managers"`. Default `"manager"` is the unchanged full-close behavior. `"index-managers"` releases **only** the disposable local index managers (a narrow `releaseMemoryIndexManagers` path in memory-core) and leaves the shared QMD manager alive.
- active-memory's recall-timeout cleanup now calls `closeActiveMemorySearchManager({ ..., scope: "index-managers" })`.
- No new public SDK export is added (the public callable export count is unchanged), so the prior review's `merge-risk: compatibility` (expanded plugin-facing API surface) no longer applies.

The recall's own in-flight QMD query is already cancelled via the embedded run abort signal, so the preserved manager stays healthy — it is the same long-lived manager CLI search and startup timers use successfully.

Addresses the prior review:
- **Compatibility / API surface:** reworked from a new SDK facade to a backward-compatible optional param — no export-count growth.
- **P1 (active-memory timeout test):** the timeout-cleanup test now asserts the `scope: "index-managers"` call, proving the new path.
- **P2 (absent release hook):** intentional no-op with a documenting comment — falling back to full-close would reintroduce #96455.

## User Impact

Users with `active-memory` enabled and a QMD-backed `memory_search` no longer lose chat memory recall after an active-memory recall timeout. The disposable local index/embedding cleanup from #84048 is preserved; only the over-broad shared-manager teardown is removed. No config or API removal; one additive optional param on an existing facade.

## Evidence

Reworked on latest `upstream/main`. Single commit; +134 / -11 across 13 files; SDK callable-export surface unchanged.

### Real QMD-backed runtime proof (qmd 2.5.3)

Installed real `@tobilu/qmd` 2.5.3 (BM25 `searchMode: "search"`). Drove the **real, un-mocked** memory-core `FallbackMemoryManager` (QMD-backed) through the recall-timeout cleanup path vs the full close:

```
[proof] manager#1: FallbackMemoryManager error: (none)
[proof] BEFORE cleanup: search outcome: {"ok":true,"hits":0}
[proof] AFTER releaseMemoryIndexManagers: sameManager: true
[proof] AFTER cleanup: search outcome: {"ok":true,"hits":0}
[proof] AFTER full close: old-manager search outcome: {"ok":false,"message":"memory search manager is closed"}
[proof] after close, fresh get is new manager: true
```

- After the recall-timeout release path: the **same** shared manager is preserved and `search` stays usable — no `"memory search manager is closed"`.
- After a full close (the old behavior): the manager throws exactly `"memory search manager is closed"` — the issue's literal symptom — and a fresh manager is created.

Real qmd BM25 retrieval is healthy on the manager's own index (the `hits:0` above is a separate qmd multi-collection/BM25-scoring nuance on the manager's filtered CLI path, unrelated to manager liveness):

```
$ qmd search "Falcon launch"   # against the manager's own index.sqlite
qmd://memory-root-main/MEMORY.md:3 #e5bc2f
Title: Memory

Project Falcon launch window opens in March. The methalox engine passed static fire.
```

### Regression tests

```
extensions/memory-core/src/memory/search-manager.test.ts ... 37 passed
src/plugins/memory-runtime.test.ts ........................ 12 passed (incl. scope-routing)
src/plugin-sdk/memory-host-search.test.ts ................. 4 passed  (incl. scope passthrough)
extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts ................... 152 passed (timeout asserts scope)
test/scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.test.ts ............. 7 passed  (callable-export budget unchanged)
```

- `search-manager.test.ts`: full-close still closes the QMD manager + new "releaseMemoryIndexManagers ... keeps the shared qmd manager alive (#96455)".
- `memory-runtime.test.ts`: routes `scope: "index-managers"` to the release path without retiring the shared manager.

### Local validation

```
tsgo:core                          OK
oxlint (changed files)             clean
oxfmt --check (changed files)      clean
plugin-sdk:api:gen + :check        OK (no new callable export)
```

target.title: fix(control-ui): improve session key display for direct chats with long identifiers
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Long session identifiers (>20 chars) in direct chat session keys overflow the sessions table and detail panel, making the Control UI hard to read
- The hover tooltip shows the same truncated value, losing the full session key context

Why does this matter now?

- Issue #61213: Users with Feishu/long-ID channels see keys like `agent:main:feishu:direct:ou_67075ec667cac0a7feae2c5094fd27b2` in every cell, wasting horizontal space

What is the intended outcome?

- Long identifiers are truncated to `first_6...last_4` format in visible cells
- The full raw session key is preserved in the hover/tooltip for copy/debug access (fallback rows only)
- Sessions with identity labels keep hover = visible label (preserves existing behavior)
- Sessions with displayNames are unchanged (displayName stays as secondary, never promoted to main cell)
- The canonical `parseSessionKey` function remains unchanged; truncation only happens via the new `shortenSessionKeyForCell` helper

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No changes to the chat page session picker, sidebar, or overview cards
- No changes to the `parseSessionKey` / `resolveSessionDisplayName` APIs
- No runtime config or user preference for truncation length

What does success look like?

- `agent:main:feishu:direct:ou_67075ec667cac0a7feae2c5094fd27b2` renders as `Feishu · ou_670...27b2` in the cell
- Hovering on fallback rows (no identity) shows the full raw key
- Hovering on identity rows shows the visible label (friendlyKeyLabel)
- `agent:main:telegram:direct:user_123` (short) renders as `Telegram · user_123` unchanged
- All 47 related unit tests pass

What should reviewers focus on?

- `ui/src/ui/session-display.ts`: new `shortenSessionKeyForCell` function (lines 31-50)
- `ui/src/ui/views/sessions.ts:840-846`: `hoverTitle` and `keyCellTitle` logic
- `ui/src/ui/session-display.test.ts`: 21 tests covering both canonical and cell-specific behavior

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Related to #61213 (partial fix - truncation implemented, binding.comment and agent.name precedence out of scope)

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

- PR #94813 (this PR)

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No; self-identified contributor fix

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Long session identifiers overflow the sessions table cells in the Control UI. The raw key is now only visible on hover for fallback rows, not in the cell text.

**Real environment tested:** Local checkout, `ui` directory vitest project.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
# Unit tests for the new helper
cd ui && npx vitest run src/ui/session-display.test.ts

# Existing sessions UI regression tests
cd ui && npx vitest run src/ui/views/sessions.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```
✓ src/ui/session-display.test.ts (21 tests)
✓ src/ui/views/sessions.test.ts (26 tests)
```

- `parseSessionKey("agent:main:feishu:direct:ou_67075ec667cac0a7feae2c5094fd27b2")` returns full identifier (canonical)
- `shortenSessionKeyForCell("agent:main:feishu:direct:ou_67075ec667cac0a7feae2c5094fd27b2")` returns `"Feishu · ou_670...27b2"` (truncated)
- Hover on fallback rows: raw key; Hover on identity rows: visible label

**Screenshots:** See previous comments.

**Observed result after fix:** Long session identifiers like `agent:main:feishu:direct:ou_67075ec667cac0a7feae2c5094fd27b2` now display as `Feishu · ou_670...27b2` in the sessions table cells. Hovering on fallback rows (no identity) shows the full raw key in the tooltip. Hovering on identity rows shows the friendly label. Short identifiers (<=20 chars) remain unchanged.

**What was not tested:** Live runtime proof with actual Feishu/Telegram/long-ID channel sessions in a running OpenClaw instance. This change was validated via unit tests only (47 tests passing). The UI truncation logic is visually verified through existing test assertions on the rendered output.

## Tests

```bash
# New + existing unit tests
cd ui && npx vitest run src/ui/session-display.test.ts src/ui/views/sessions.test.ts
# → 47 passed
```

## Risk

| Question | Yes / No |
|---|---|
| Does this change affect an existing public API (config, CLI, plugin SDK, gateway protocol)? | No |
| Does this change affect an upgrade path or require a migration? | No |
| Could this change break existing user workflows or persisted state? | No — pure UI display change, no state or config changes |
| Does this change introduce a new dependency or modify an existing one? | No |

**Risk description:** Low risk. Pure frontend display change with dedicated regression tests.

**Mitigations:** 
- Canonical `parseSessionKey` unchanged (5 existing consumers unaffected)
- `shortenSessionKeyForCell` is new and scoped to Sessions table only
- displayName stays secondary, never promoted to main cell
- Identity rows preserve existing hover behavior

## Current review state

- [x] Code changes complete
- [x] Unit tests: 21 new + 26 existing = 47 passing
- [x] Format check: all files correct
- [ ] Reviewer: awaiting maintainer review

commands.author: gbharg

target.title: docs: make workflow tracking GitHub-native
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Repository workflow guidance did not explicitly state that OpenClaw repo work should use GitHub-native tracking by default, which left room for private external trackers such as Linear to be treated as required context.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds a root policy line that GitHub issues/PRs are the default tracking surface and clarifies the PR template comment so external tracker links are optional extra context, not required closure fields.

## User Impact

Contributors and agents can prepare OpenClaw PRs without private Linear access. Existing external tracker links can still be included when already available or explicitly requested.

## Evidence

Local verification:

```text
git diff HEAD^..HEAD --check
git grep -n -E 'Linear issue|Linear ticket|Linear is the default|linear-required|Create a Linear|Link Linear|linear_issue|AI-XXX|US-YYY' HEAD -- AGENTS.md .github .agents skills scripts docs
```

Results: whitespace check passed, and the targeted search returned no active mandatory Linear-tracking hits.

Branch hygiene: rebased onto current `upstream/main`; final diff is one commit touching only `AGENTS.md` and `.github/pull_request_template.md`.
target.author: gbharg

target.title: feat(workboard): add workboard_delete tool and CLI command for card removal
target.body:
## Summary

Adds `workboard_delete` agent tool and `openclaw workboard delete` CLI command to permanently remove Workboard cards. Closes #92314.

**Problem:** The Workboard plugin has `WorkboardStore.delete()` and a gateway method `workboard.cards.delete`, but no agent-facing tool or CLI command. Agents cannot permanently remove cards.

**Changes:**
- **`tools.ts`**: Add `workboard_delete` tool with claim-scope authorization. Unclaimed cards can be deleted by any agent; claimed cards require the claim owner or a valid claim token.
- **`cli.ts`**: Add `openclaw workboard delete <id>` command with id-prefix resolution and `--json` output.
- **`tools.test.ts`**: Three new tests — delete unclaimed card with reference cleanup, claim-scope enforcement, and nonexistent-card error.

**Authorization model:** Follows the existing `canMutateCard` pattern used by `workboard_heartbeat`, `workboard_release`, etc. Hard-deletes the card row (with `ON DELETE CASCADE` on all child tables) plus cleans up KV-stored subscriptions, attachments, and cross-card link references via the existing `removeReferencesToCard` path.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Agents can permanently delete Workboard cards through the `workboard_delete` tool. Claimed cards require claim ownership. Cross-card references are cleaned up.

**Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node.js 24.x, pnpm, upstream `main` at c1dca304.

**Steps run after the patch:**
1. Branch from `upstream/main`, apply changes.
2. Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts` to verify all 9 tool tests pass.
3. Run `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/workboard/src/store.test.ts extensions/workboard/src/gateway.test.ts extensions/workboard/src/command.test.ts` to verify no regressions.

**Evidence after fix:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts

 ✓  extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts (9 tests) 48ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)
   Duration  1.43s
```

**Observed result after fix:** All 9 tool tests pass including the 3 new delete tests. The `workboard_delete` tool correctly deletes unclaimed cards, enforces claim scope on claimed cards, throws for nonexistent cards, and cleans up parent-card link references.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end gateway dispatch, management UI delete button (not in scope).

commands.author: ruizcrp

target.title: feat(workboard): add move command to CLI and workboard_move tool
target.body:
What Problem This Solves

Workboard agents and operators can create, claim, and complete cards, but there was no direct way to move a card from one column (status) to another. Agents had to rely on the dispatch command or manual store mutations to transition cards, which is awkward for deliberate column changes like "moving a card to Review" or "sending it back to Triage."

Why This Change Was Made

The store already had a move(id, status, position) method, but it was never exposed to the CLI or the agent tools layer. This change wires it up in both directions:

• CLI: workboard move <id> --status <status> for operators and agents running in the terminal
• Tool: workboard_move(id, status, token?) for agent tool use, with claim-scoped access control (only agents that have claimed the card can move it, matching the pattern used by reassign and release)

Status values are validated against the WORKBOARD_STATUSES enum at the tool layer before reaching the store, so invalid statuses are rejected early with a clear error message.

User Impact

• Agents can now move cards between columns as part of their workflow (e.g., moving a card to review after completing implementation)
• Operators can manually move cards via the CLI without writing store queries
• Both CLI and tool outputs support --json for machine-readable consumption

I want to underline that the code was written with AI-assistance. But I went over the code and checked it manually

The following evidence comes from manually conducting terminal runs.  I am limiting the outputs to the relevant lines.

First I create a new card
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard create card-abc123 --status tod
acc31be5  todo      normal  default  card-abc123

Here is a list to show the status on the board before moving a card
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard list
acc31be5  todo      normal  default  card-abc123

OK, now the moving comes:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status running
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

List again to see:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard list
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

Show details:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard show acc31be5
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

Try moving again:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status review
acc31be5  review    normal  default  card-abc123

Try moving back. With JSON:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status triage --json
{
  "card": {
    "id": "acc31be5-2e01-4dd0-93a9-5999779d9a30",
    "title": "card-abc123",
    "status": "triage",
    "priority": "normal",
    "labels": [],
    "position": 1000,
    "createdAt": 1782332679684,
    "updatedAt": 1782333474509,
    "startedAt": 1782333120149,
    "events": [
      {
        "id": "efb13f78-423f-4467-a443-6d61881f8433",
        "kind": "created",
        "at": 1782332679684,
        "toStatus": "todo"
      },
      {
        "id": "df656eb0-3c22-4998-b3db-934e3931836b",
        "kind": "moved",
        "at": 1782333120149,
        "fromStatus": "todo",
        "toStatus": "running"
      },
      {
        "id": "e9446e73-521a-41b8-bffe-16c8a9efde59",
        "kind": "moved",
        "at": 1782333379120,
        "fromStatus": "running",
        "toStatus": "review"
      },
      {
        "id": "1f72ee1f-3736-4269-9c95-b6411a08a7db",
        "at": 1782333474509,
        "kind": "moved",
        "fromStatus": "review
        "toStatus": "triage"
      }
    ]
  }
}

What happens if the status is not valid?
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status nonexistant
Error: status must be one of: triage, backlog, todo, scheduled, ready, running, review, blocked, done.
Exactly, we want a reasoned error if the column does not exist.

What if the --status flag is missing?
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5
Error: --status is required. Use: workboard move <id> --status <status>
This is also correct: a status is necessary to move the card to a target column.

Tests were also conducted:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/clawdbot/openclaw
 ✓  extensions  extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts (7 tests) 350ms
 ✓  extensions  extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts (10 tests) 55ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  17 passed (17)


There is no linked issue for this PR.

@clawsweeper please have a look at it.

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: fix(barnacle): resync candidate labels on ready_for_review, remove stale labels
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Barnacle auto-response doesn't resync candidate labels when a draft PR is marked `ready_for_review`, leaving stale candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) on PRs with meaningful bodies.
- Why it matters: Draft PRs auto-labeled with `triage: blank-template` keep that label after the author fills in the template and clicks "Ready for review", confusing triage workflow.
- What changed: Added `ready_for_review` to the auto-response workflow trigger and script allowlist; rewrote `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` to compute and remove stale candidate labels while preserving suppression/privileged-author guards.
- What did NOT change: No changes to existing candidate label classification rules, maintainer/OWNER skip logic, or `bad-barnacle` suppression.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #77343
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Three gaps — (1) `.github/workflows/auto-response.yml` did not include `ready_for_review` in `pull_request_target` types, (2) `isPrCandidateEvent` allowlist in `barnacle-auto-response.mjs` did not include `ready_for_review`, and (3) `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` was add-only (never removed stale labels).
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test for the `ready_for_review` → stale label removal path.
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Real behavior proof

Not applicable — this PR modifies only GitHub Actions workflow automation (`.github/workflows/auto-response.yml`).
No product code, CLI, gateway, plugin, or UI surface is touched.
Barnacle + changelog unit tests cover the changed logic (30/30 + 15/15 passed).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `test/scripts/barnacle-auto-response.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `ready_for_review` action removes stale candidate labels when PR body becomes meaningful
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Directly exercises `applyPullRequestCandidateLabels` return value + `removeLabels` call path
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — this was a new coverage gap
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

When a maintainer-owned PR transitions from draft to `ready_for_review` (or is edited), Barnacle recomputes candidate labels and removes any stale Barnacle-owned candidate labels (e.g. `triage: blank-template`) that no longer match the PR body/files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [add missing labels only] -> [stale labels persist]

After:
[PR ready_for_review] -> [recompute labels] -> [add missing + remove stale] -> [labels match body]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No (existing GitHub API calls)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any (GitHub Actions)
- Runtime/container: Node 24 (GitHub Actions)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: GitHub auto-response workflow

### Steps

1. Open a draft PR with a blank issue template body
2. Barnacle auto-labels it with `triage: blank-template`
3. Edit body to fill in the template (Problem/Why/What)
4. Click "Ready for review"

### Expected

- `triage: blank-template` is removed; correct candidate labels applied

### Actual

- `triage: blank-template` persisted (before fix)

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `ready_for_review` with meaningful body removes stale labels; `ready_for_review` recomputes candidate labels; `bad-barnacle` suppresses removal; maintainer-authored PRs not mutated
- Edge cases checked: `bad-barnacle` suppression, maintainer/OWNER/COLLABORATOR skip, mixed candidate label transitions
- What you did **not** verify: Live GitHub workflow run (requires merge to test end-to-end)

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Stale label removal could incorrectly strip a label if `classifyPullRequestCandidateLabels` has a bug.
  - Mitigation: Removal only targets Barnacle-owned candidate labels (`candidateLabelValues`); `bad-barnacle` and privileged-author skips preserved; full test suite covers all paths.

target.title: fix(trajectory): export legacy v1 sessions without entry timestamps
target.body:
## Summary

Exporting a trajectory bundle for an old (version 1) session produces an empty transcript. The CLI reports `Trajectory exported!` and writes a bundle whose `manifest.transcriptEventCount` is `0`, silently dropping every message in the session. This hits real users: `openclaw export-trajectory` / `/export-trajectory` run against any session file written before entry timestamps existed.

## Root cause

`readSessionBranch` filters session entries down to canonical transcript entries, but the filter also requires a present `timestamp`:

```ts
// before - src/trajectory/export.ts
const entries = fileEntries.filter(
  (entry): entry is SessionEntry =>
    entry.type !== "session" &&
    isCanonicalSessionTranscriptEntry(entry) &&
    typeof (entry as { id?: unknown }).id === "string" &&
    (typeof (entry as { timestamp?: unknown }).timestamp === "string" ||
      typeof (entry as { timestamp?: unknown }).timestamp === "number"),
);
```

The legacy migration `migrateLegacySessionEntries` synthesizes an `id` and `parentId` for every entry of a `version < 2` session, but it never synthesizes a `timestamp` (those logs predate entry timestamps). The downstream event builder already tolerates a missing timestamp: `buildTranscriptEvents` calls `normalizeTimestamp(entry.timestamp)`, which defaults an absent value to the epoch. That tolerance is dead code, because the filter strips the timestamp-less entries before they ever reach it. Result: every entry of a timestamp-less v1 session is filtered out and the bundle is empty.

## Fix

Drop the timestamp clause from the filter. Entry identity (`id`) plus `isCanonicalSessionTranscriptEntry` is what the branch walk needs; the timestamp is normalized later.

```ts
// after - src/trajectory/export.ts
const entries = fileEntries.filter(
  (entry): entry is SessionEntry =>
    entry.type !== "session" &&
    isCanonicalSessionTranscriptEntry(entry) &&
    typeof (entry as { id?: unknown }).id === "string",
);
```

`normalizeTimestamp` (already in the event path) defaults a missing or non-finite timestamp to `new Date(0).toISOString()`, so v3 sessions with real timestamps are unaffected and v1 sessions now export with epoch-defaulted timestamps instead of vanishing.

## Why this is the right boundary

`readSessionBranch` is the single owner of which persisted entries enter the exported branch. The timestamp requirement contradicted the migration contract (v1 entries legitimately have no entry timestamp) and the normalization contract (the builder already defaults missing timestamps). Removing the clause makes those two contracts reachable instead of adding a second compensating path. No sibling surface re-derives this filter; runtime event ingestion is separate and uses its own `isRuntimeTrajectoryEvent` guard, which is unchanged.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/trajectory/export.test.ts` - full 24-test export suite green with the fix (23 existing + 1 new regression).
- New test `exports the transcript for a legacy v1 session without entry timestamps` fails on pristine `main` (`transcriptEventCount` is `0`) and passes with the patch.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/trajectory/export.ts src/trajectory/export.test.ts` clean.
- `oxfmt --check` clean on both files.
- `tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json` and `-p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json` clean.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: trajectory export of a legacy v1 session (no entry timestamps) drops the entire transcript and reports `transcriptEventCount: 0`.
Real environment tested: the real `exportTrajectoryBundle` runtime driven end to end over a v1 session JSONL (header `version: 1`, two timestamp-less message entries), on pristine `main` and on the patched tree with identical input.

## Summary

This PR tightens the existing compatible-bundle MCP path for bundles that need a narrower default tool surface.

- Adds regression coverage that a Codex-compatible bundle `.mcp.json` preserves `toolFilter.include`, `toolFilter.exclude`, and a relative bundle data root.
- Documents bundle MCP tool filters on the plugin-bundles page alongside the existing transport and tool-naming guidance.
- Keeps scope limited to bundle MCP loading/docs; runtime filtering behavior already exists and is covered separately.

Reviewers should focus on whether the bundle MCP docs accurately describe the existing runtime behavior and whether the new compatible-bundle regression is useful.

## Linked context

No issue filed.

Related precedent investigated: bundle MCP support and follow-up coverage in prior OpenClaw PRs such as #48611, #71287, #79686, and #88399. This PR follows that existing bundle MCP seam.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Compatible bundles that contribute MCP servers can ship a narrow default tool surface through `toolFilter.include` and `toolFilter.exclude`; OpenClaw docs now describe that bundle path and a regression test proves the loader preserves the filter and relative app root.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `test/filtered-bundle-mcp`, commit `d5bffb3dc0`, with an isolated temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary Codex-compatible bundle containing `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `skills/`, and `apps/`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins install --link <temp>/vision-mcp`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins inspect vision-mcp --json`; then loaded bundle MCP config with `node --import tsx` calling `loadEnabledBundleMcpConfig(...)` against the isolated home/config.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the temporary real OpenClaw setup:

```text

      "vision_map.commit_*",
      "vision_map.harvest_session"

}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw linked the temporary compatible bundle, detected its bundle MCP server, preserved both include and exclude filter arrays, set `cwd` to the bundle root, and resolved `./apps` to an absolute path inside the installed bundle.

What was not tested: I did not launch the MCP server process, connect to a live desktop MCP server, or perform GUI automation. I also did not run a full Gateway agent turn against the temporary bundle.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses a temporary local compatible bundle and the real OpenClaw plugin installer/loader, but the MCP server command itself is not started because this PR changes documentation and loader regression coverage, not MCP runtime execution behavior.

## Tests and validation


Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime change. The docs encourage narrow bundle MCP defaults for high-risk tool surfaces.
